---

Hey, let's talk about a concept called retrading. When you get into the real estate game, you're going to discover that retrading happens and hopefully it won't surprise you. Retrading is when unanticipated changes happen, adjusting the terms previously agreed to. This can happen to you as a seller where the buyer retraids you or you can retrade the seller, thus asking for some adjustments in the pricing of the terms. Typically retrading happens when price changes for one reason or another or the negative retrading happens when some buyer simply uses the retrade as a way to get a reduction in price after the due diligence or late in the due diligence and prior to closing the deal. It's not uncommon during the due diligence phase for you the buyer to find reasons to lower the price or better way to say that keep the price the same but get some type of allowance for the roof or the issues or whatever might have been found out in your DD. So at this point in the transaction you would have done your due diligence. We've already agreed on a purchase and sale agreement. We have not yet acquired our financing or closed the deal and you might go to the seller and say look I need 500 grand because I found out or better example when I found out about the polybutylene that they didn't disclose by the way I went back to them and said look leave the price the same give me a million dollars in escrow to escrow or you got to adjust the price. So I give people a choice. Now the broker and the seller thought I was retraiting them. They even said oh you're retraining us like it's a bad thing. Look there's a good retrade a legitimate retrade and there's that retrade that has the intention of just doing it as a mere means of reducing the price. If you spot something some massive issue of $500,000 or $50,000 issue depending upon the price of the property 500 grand on five million is significant. $50,000 on a five million dollar property not so significant but had the seller not disclosed a retrade might be in order. If you find this out in the due diligence then you'd want to go to the seller and say hey look we got to discuss the price here. Now a little about my thoughts on retraiting very simply I don't retrade. I don't want to retrade. I do everything possible not to go back to the seller and ask them for a discount. There have been situations where I've had to. For instance I was recently buying a deal in North Florida or North Miami. We found out that the seller had doctored pictures on Google of the roof. The roof had had problems and it was covered up with paint. They actually went up there and painted the roof and we were told that they had been maintaining the roof and that the roof was fine and that in fact it had another seven or eight years of warranty left on it. Which by the way was true. What they didn't tell us was they had been covering up constant problems with the roof and painted it to cover it up. So literally in the last week of our due diligence before we went hard and became non refundable I went to the seller and said look you either need to adjust the price by $850,000 we got three bids. One was for $850,000, one was for $940,000 and one was for $1,050,000 for a brand new roof. I either need you to adjust the price by the $850,000 the minimum amount, the lowest bid or I need you to put that money in escrow until I use it. The seller got extremely agitated. Very very emotional. The broker almost lost his mind and became like a 15 year old claiming that I was retraiting them unnecessarily, that I was being sneaky and slippery and I'm like guys put the $850,000 in an escrow account. After closing if I have not started putting a new roof on the building in the 30 days you get the $850,000 back. Either do that or reduce the price or cancel the deal. You didn't tell me about the roof in this way. Now they thought I was retraiting them but I knew my intention is one I'm not going to retrade number two. I'll walk away from a deal if you're not straight with me. I tell sellers and brokers that up front look I don't retrade unless I have to. If I have to retrade it will only be because you didn't disclose something up front or you disclose something that was not true. Think about the other contracts in your life. If you get married and your spouse and yourself have an agreement but then those agreements get broken or changed do you not renegotiate? Of course you do. You have to or you won't stay together. Do you renegotiate the agreement that the two of you had when you first got married and 10 years later and 20 years later if you don't renegotiate those agreements made when you were a kid you're not going to be married when you're adults. Same thing with partners. Same thing with any kind of business relationship with a customer or a customer with you or when you're the customer and they're the seller. Contracts must be renegotiated in order for them to continue to be survival for both parties. This is the same with real estate. There are those situations where you must. You need to renegotiate or if you don't you will simply either go broke or lose money in deals. Back to the situation with the roof. I suggested that there was eight years of warranty left on the roof so you might wonder well why wasn't the roof covered by the eight years of warranty left? Because they're only going to cover eight out of I think 40 years it was a 40 year warranty. They're only going to give me the money for the eight years left on the warranty and I didn't want to file a claim I just wanted my money so I could put on a new roof. Which by the way we put on a new roof on the property in the first 30 days and the seller called and said hey I got to apologize to you I thought you were just trying to pull one over on me. And remember I was more than fair with this seller because I could have gone for the $1.1 million roof but I didn't. I was fair. So be fair when you retrade okay. Have a reason to do it. Don't do it with the intention of just screwing somebody over because that reputation will get around in this very small tight brokerage community and amongst the handful of people that control multifamily in the country there's not a lot of us and when the word gets around it will defame your reputation. Now with that information that you have something legitimate that needs to be retraded you go back to the seller and explain what your situation is. Tell them you still want to close. Tell them that you're not there to renegotiate the terms. Tell them that your intention is to close that you're not trying to pull one over on them and you want to get your deal done. Of course that seller was not thrilled in the beginning especially because I told him up front dude I don't retrade and I'm not gonna and I won't but then I found this out. Okay. It's like feel felt and found you know when somebody says they have a problem you're like I man I understand I totally feel the same exact way. I felt that way until I found out right. I'm saying that because I don't want anybody to retrade me. So protect yourself when you're the seller know that the retrade can happen and when you're the buyer know how to use the retrade to your advantage. The fact is that seller and the broker both knew about the roof situation and by the way that's why they blew up so hard because they were hiding their little eyes and then blamed and projected on the media that I was doing something wrong when in fact they were the sneaky burgers not me. Okay. All right. Now there were two options on the table. Remember I could lower the price always use that. You can lower the price or you can literally give me the amount of money put it in escrow. Escrow is a third party holds the money and and don't I'll use that money from that escrow. If I don't use it then you get it back again. I don't retrade unless I have to remember that line and you should not retrade unless you have to contracts are not a game leading someone down a path and assuring them everything's good the terms are great price is great. Okay. And then not having any intention of following through on those terms will kill you. Don't be that person. Follow through on your agreements quit thinking small 50 grand on a five million dollar deal is almost immeasurable. I'm not saying you shouldn't pay attention to those things. I do. Okay. I was recently on a deal. I thought the property because it was brand new came with a wall in a gate when we closed like right before we were closing we found out that we thought on our side that they weren't finished with the production of the fence in the gate. They said no it doesn't come with the fence in the gate. I insisted on a fencing gate because I knew it was the right thing to do and it was literally a 50 million dollar deal and the fencing gate were about 150 grand. It doesn't seem like much but in this case it was the right thing to do because a new property should come with a fence in a gate and the seller should have shared with me the roofing situation. The seller in Orlando should have shared with me we're done as is no more additions we're finished but this was work in progress. So look we had to retrade no other way to say it. My intentions weren't to hurt though. One final note on the retrade when you encounter a situation where there needs to be a retrade dig deeper if the seller has not disclosed something like I described earlier what else haven't they disclosed? What else have they lied about? What else did they not tell you about? Look deeper. You don't want to be left paying too much and later spending too much to get the building where it needs to be in order to operate it. That's what good investors do and that's why good investors don't lose money.
